question: Explain why transition elements have variable oxidation states.
full_answer: Transition elements have variable oxidation states because the 3d and 4s subshells are close in energy. Therefore different numbers of electrons can be used in bonding or removed.

question: Explain what is meant by a bidentate ligand.
full_answer: A bidentate ligand has two atoms with lone pairs available for donation. It forms two dative covalent bonds to the same metal ion.

question: Explain the trend in the solubilities of the Group 2 sulfates.
full_answer: The solubilities decrease down the group because both lattice enthalpy and hydration enthalpy become less exothermic. The hydration enthalpy decreases more than the lattice enthalpy, so dissolving becomes less favourable.

question: Explain why a measured Ecell may be less positive than E°cell.
full_answer: Standard electrode potentials apply only under standard conditions. If the concentrations are not 1 mol dm-3, the electrode potentials change. Therefore the measured Ecell may be less positive than E°cell.

question: Explain why adding Ni2+ to the iodate system makes Ecell less positive.
full_answer: Adding Ni2+ shifts the equilibrium for dissolving nickel iodate to the left by the common ion effect. This decreases the iodate concentration, so the reduction potential falls and Ecell becomes less positive.

question: Explain why an O-H peak disappears after shaking with D2O.
full_answer: The O-H proton is exchangeable. It is replaced by deuterium from D2O, and deuterium is not detected in the same way in a proton NMR spectrum. Therefore the O-H peak disappears.

question: Explain why it may be important to make only one optical isomer of a drug.

question: Describe the trend in the thermal stabilities of the carbonates of the Group 2 elements. Explain your answer.
full_answer: The thermal stability of the Group 2 carbonates increases down the group. The M2+ ions become larger and have lower charge density, so they polarise the carbonate ion less. The carbonate ion is less distorted and less likely to decompose.

question: Explain why benzene is less likely than an alkene to undergo addition reactions.
full_answer: Benzene has a delocalised pi system and is stabilised by aromaticity. An addition reaction would break this delocalisation, so addition is less favourable than substitution.

rounds.prompt: Benzene has a _____ pi system and is stabilised by aromaticity. An addition reaction would break this delocalisation, so addition is less favourable than substitution.
